factors.summary: Free cash flow is negative due to significant working capital increases, leading to a $50.7 million cash outflow from operations.

evidence.text: Cash used in operating activities was $50.7 million for the three months ended March 31, 2020 compared to cash provided by operating activities of $6.1 million for the three months ended March 31, 2019. The $56.8 million decrease in cash provided by operations was largely the result of our working capital increase of $125.1 million in the first three months of 2020, exceeding the working capital increase of $64.2 million for the first three months of 2019.

factors.summary: Labor costs are increasing, driven by higher compensation expenses and rising insurance costs.

evidence.text: Selling, general and administrative expenses increased $34.4 million, or 9.3%. This increase was primarily due to higher compensation expense related to the increase in net sales and rising insurance expenses. Higher depreciation and amortization expense also contributed to the increase.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended March 31, 2020 Compared with the Three Months Ended March 31, 2019

factors.summary: Pricing power is being challenged due to COVID-19 impacts.

evidence.text: The extent and duration of this disruption and uncertainty are yet to be fully known, and we expect to experience a decline in housing starts, reduced sales demand, increased margin pressures and increased operating costs as a result.

factors.summary: Cost actions are being taken to manage expenses.

evidence.text: We will continue to focus on working capital by closely monitoring the credit exposure of our customers, remaining focused on maintaining the right level of inventory and by working with our vendors to improve payment terms and pricing on our products. We strive to achieve the appropriate balance of short-term expense control while maintaining the expertise and capacity to grow the business as market conditions improve.

factors.summary: Inventory levels are being managed carefully.

evidence.text: We will continue to focus on working capital by closely monitoring the credit exposure of our customers, remaining focused on maintaining the right level of inventory and by working with our vendors to improve payment terms and pricing on our products.

factors.summary: Management is cautious due to the impact of COVID-19 but remains optimistic about long-term growth.

evidence.text: While temporarily hampered by the disruptions and uncertainties brought on by the COVID-19 pandemic, we believe the long-term outlook for the housing industry is positive due to growth in the underlying demographics. We feel we are well-positioned to take advantage of the construction activity in our markets and to increase our market share, which may include strategic acquisitions.

factors.summary: Near-term risks include disruptions from COVID-19 and economic uncertainties.

evidence.text: The extent and duration of this disruption and uncertainty are yet to be fully known, and we expect to experience a decline in housing starts, reduced sales demand, increased margin pressures and increased operating costs as a result. In addition to the COVID-19 effect, we may continue to experience pressure on our gross margins due to lower levels of housing starts versus historical norms, increased competition for homebuilder business and cyclical fluctuations in commodity prices.

factors.summary: Long-term strategy remains focused on growth through market position and strategic acquisitions.

evidence.text: We believe the long-term outlook for the housing industry is positive due to growth in the underlying demographics. We feel we are well-positioned to take advantage of the construction activity in our markets and to increase our market share, which may include strategic acquisitions.

factors.summary: Key sensitivities include housing affordability, lumber prices, and economic conditions.

evidence.text: Our operating results are dependent on the following trends, events and uncertainties, some of which are beyond our control: Homebuilding Industry; Repair and remodel end market; Use of Prefabricated Components; Economic Conditions; Housing Affordability; Cost of Materials; Controlling Expenses; Multi-Family and Light Commercial Business; Capital Structure.

factors.summary: Catalysts include strategic acquisitions and market recovery post-COVID-19.

evidence.text: We will continue to identify opportunities for profitable growth in the multi-family and light commercial markets. Our net sales for the first quarter of 2020 increased 9.5% from the same period last year. Acquisitions accounted for 3.5% of our sales growth in the first quarter of 2020, while commodity price inflation and one more selling day increased our sales by 0.4% and 1.7%, respectively.

factors.summary: Market share is a focus area, with efforts to maintain and grow it.

evidence.text: Our focus is on maintaining relationships and market share with these customers while balancing the competitive pressures we are facing in servicing large homebuilders with certain profitability expectations. Additionally, we have been successful in expanding our custom homebuilder base while maintaining acceptable credit standards.

factors.summary: Competitive dynamics are intense with consolidation and fragmented markets.

evidence.text: The homebuilding industry has undergone consolidation, and the larger homebuilders have increased their market share. While it is uncertain how long demand will be disrupted due to the current pandemic, we still believe there are several meaningful trends that indicate U.S. housing demand will rebound and continue to trend towards recovering to the historical average. These trends include relatively low interest rates, the aging of housing stock and normal population growth due to immigration and birth rate exceeding death rate.

factors.summary: Differentiation is achieved through integrated solutions and product offerings.

evidence.text: We offer an integrated solution to our customers providing manufacturing, supply and installation of a full range of structural and related building products. Our manufactured products include our factory-built roof and floor trusses, wall panels and stairs, vinyl windows, custom millwork and trim, as well as engineered wood that we design, cut, and assemble for each home.

factors.summary: Construction infrastructure spending is expected to support demand despite current disruptions.

evidence.text: While the level of housing starts remains below the historical average, the homebuilding industry has improved since 2011. However, in March of 2020, the U.S. economy began to see significant disruption and uncertainty from the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic. While the extent of these disruptions and uncertainties is yet to be fully known, we expect to experience reduced sales demand, increased margin pressures and increased operating costs as a result.

factors.summary: Debt leverage is being managed through transactions to optimize capital structure.

evidence.text: These transactions are described in Notes 4 and 13 to the condensed consolidated financial statements included in Item 1 of this quarterly report on Form 10-Q. From time to time, based on market conditions and other factors and subject to compliance with applicable laws and regulations, the Company may repurchase or call our notes, repay debt, repurchase shares of our common stock or otherwise enter into transactions regarding its capital structure.

factors.summary: Demand trends are mixed, with some positive signs despite disruptions from COVID-19.

evidence.text: We achieved increased sales in all our product categories, except in our Gypsum, roofing and insulation category, primarily due to higher sales volumes and as a result of our continued efforts to focus on higher margin opportunities.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended March 31, 2020 Compared with the Three Months Ended March 31, 2019

evidence.text: While the level of housing starts remains below the historical average, the homebuilding industry has improved since 2011. However, in March of 2020, the U.S. economy began to see significant disruption and uncertainty from the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic.

factors.summary: Organic growth is present, with core organic growth contributing to net sales increases in several product categories and segments. Organic revenue growth is positive, with core organic sales growth achieved despite challenges.

evidence.text: Core organic growth increased net sales by 3.9% and acquisitions contributed 3.5% compared to 2019. We achieved increased sales in all our product categories, except in our Gypsum, roofing and insulation category.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended March 31, 2020 Compared with the Three Months Ended March 31, 2019

evidence.text: Excluding the impact of acquisitions, commodity price inflation and the impact of one more selling day, we achieved 3.9% core organic sales growth in the single-family, multi-family and repair and remodel/other end markets.

factors.summary: Volume trends show mixed performance, with overall unit volume growth driven by increased sales across most product categories, but constrained by market conditions and some category-specific declines.

evidence.text: We achieved increased sales in all our product categories, except in our Gypsum, roofing and insulation category, primarily due to higher sales volumes and as a result of our continued efforts to focus on higher margin opportunities.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended March 31, 2020 Compared with the Three Months Ended March 31, 2019

evidence.text: Our net sales for the first quarter of 2020 increased 9.5% from the same period last year. Acquisitions accounted for 3.5% of our sales growth in the first quarter of 2020, while commodity price inflation and one more selling day increased our sales by 0.4% and 1.7%, respectively. Excluding the impact of acquisitions, commodity price inflation and the impact of one more selling day, we achieved 3.9% core organic sales growth in the single-family, multi-family and repair and remodel/other end markets.

factors.summary: The end market momentum is mixed, with the West segment showing growth due to increased profitability and sales volume, while other regions like Northeast, Southeast, and South are facing slower growth and margin compression. Additionally, the sector has been impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, leading to expected reduced sales demand and increased margin pressures.

evidence.text: We achieved increased profitability in our West reportable segment due to increased sales volume in the first quarter of 2020 compared to the first quarter of 2019, where we experienced the impact of adverse weather conditions in the first quarter of 2019. However, profitability declined in our Northeast, Southeast and South reportable segments due to a decline in gross margin percentages in the first quarter of 2020 compared to the first quarter of 2019, where we experienced stronger gross margin performance as a result of commodity deflation in the period.

The homebuilding industry has improved since 2011. However, in March of 2020, the U.S. economy began to see significant disruption and uncertainty from the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic. While the extent of these disruptions and uncertainties is yet to be fully known, we expect to experience reduced sales demand, increased margin pressures and increased operating costs as a result.

factors.summary: Input costs are volatile, primarily influenced by rising commodity prices such as lumber, which have contributed to sales increases but also led to margin compression. The volatility is further exacerbated by the cyclical nature of the homebuilding industry and general economic conditions.

evidence.text: Commodity prices are rising, contributing to sales increases but also leading to margin compression.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended March 31, 2020 Compared with the Three Months Ended March 31, 2019

evidence.text: Further, one additional selling day increased sales by 1.7%, while commodity price inflation increased our sales in the first quarter of 2020 by 0.4%. We experienced a stronger gross margin percentage in the first quarter of 2019 as a result of commodity deflation in the period.

The volatility of lumber prices; The cyclical nature of the homebuilding industry; General economic conditions in the markets in which we compete; The pricing policies of our competitors; The production schedules of our customers; and The effects of weather.

factors.summary: The company's margins are under negative pressure, primarily due to increased costs and reduced demand. While higher sales volumes provided some cost leverage, leading to a slight improvement in gross margin dollars, the overall margin percentage decreased due to commodity price increases and higher input costs.

evidence.text: Our gross margin percentage decreased to 26.0% in the first quarter of 2020 from 27.1% in the first quarter of 2019, a 1.1% decrease. We experienced a stronger gross margin percentage in the first quarter of 2019 as a result of commodity deflation in the period. As a percentage of net sales, selling, general and administrative expenses decreased to 22.6% in the first quarter of 2020 from 22.7% in the first quarter of 2019, largely driven by cost leverage on increased sales volumes.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended March 31, 2020 Compared with the Three Months Ended March 31, 2019

evidence.text: Our gross profit percentage in the first quarter of 2020 decreased by 1.1% compared to the first quarter of 2019 where we experienced stronger gross margin percentage as a result of commodity deflation in the period. In addition, sales volume growth in the majority of our product categories contributed to increased gross margin dollars compared to the first quarter of 2019.

factors.summary: The economic conditions are facing significant challenges due to the COVID-19 pandemic, leading to disruptions in supply chains, reduced demand, and increased operational pressures. The uncertainty and duration of these impacts remain unknown, with potential consequences such as declines in housing starts, margin pressures, and higher operating costs.

evidence.text: The COVID-19 pandemic could also cause disruptions in our supply chain. The inability of our suppliers to meet our supply needs in a timely manner or our quality standards could cause delays to delivery date requirements of our customers. Such failures could result in the cancellation of orders, customers’ refusal to accept deliveries, a reduction in purchase prices, and ultimately, termination of customer relationships, any of which could have a material adverse effect on our business, financial condition, results of operations and liquidity.
evidence.subsection: The outbreak of a health epidemic or pandemic, including COVID-19, may have an adverse effect on our business.

evidence.text: The U.S. economy began to see significant disruption and uncertainty from the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic. The extent and duration of this disruption and uncertainty are yet to be fully known, and we expect to experience a decline in housing starts, reduced sales demand, increased margin pressures and increased operating costs as a result.

factors.summary: The company has engaged in significant M&A activity, specifically the acquisition of Bianchi, which has increased cash used in investing activities.

evidence.text: Cash used in investing activities was $43.9 million and $20.8 million for the three months ended March 31, 2020 and 2019, respectively. This increase in cash used in investing activities was primarily due to our acquisition of Bianchi in the first quarter of 2020.

On January 9, 2020, we acquired certain assets and operations of Bianchi & Company, Inc. (''Bianchi'') for $15.9 million in cash. Located in Charlotte, North Carolina, Bianchi is a supplier and installer of interior and exterior millwork.
